serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 250 ml (250 g) of lemongrass and pandan drink, as typically served in a cup or glass at a Singaporean café.

nutrient_source.title: Lemongrass tea, brewed — estimated from USDA FoodData Central reference values for herbal tea infusions and lemongrass water
nutrient_source.organization: USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy / Foundation Foods)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for lemongrass-pandan drink exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for brewed herbal tea (lemongrass infusion, unsweetened) and cross-referenced with published compositional data for Pandanus amaryllifolius infusions. The drink is predominantly water with trace phytochemicals; macronutrients are effectively zero for the unsweetened base. A small sugar contribution (~2–3 g/100 ml) is included to reflect the lightly sweetened preparation typical of café-served versions. Values represent a lightly sweetened preparation.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of herbal infusions and low-carbohydrate beverages — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for herbal teas
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for lemongrass-pandan drink. The University of Sydney GI Database lists plain herbal teas and low-sugar botanical infusions as having negligible glycemic impact. GI is set to 1 (effectively zero) per convention for beverages with minimal glycemic carbohydrates. Glycemic load per 250 g serving is 0.0 given the very low carbohydrate content (~2.5 g/100 g, predominantly simple sugars at low quantity). This is consistent with the convention applied to essentially zero-GI beverages.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (~2 mg/100 ml); lemongrass has mild vasodilatory properties in preclinical studies
hypertension.guidance: Lemongrass and pandan drink is very low in sodium (~5 mg per 250 ml serving) and is generally suitable for individuals managing hypertension, though café-prepared versions may contain added sugar and should be requested unsweetened or lightly sweetened.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate content and negligible glycemic index; sugar content depends on preparation
type2_diabetes.guidance: The unsweetened or lightly sweetened lemongrass-pandan drink has a negligible glycemic index and low carbohydrate load, making it a suitable beverage choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, provided added sugar is minimised or omitted.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Zero fat, zero cholesterol, and low sodium; lemongrass contains antioxidant phytochemicals
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Lemongrass and pandan drink contains no fat, no cholesterol, and negligible sodium, making it a heart-friendly beverage; the antioxidant compounds in lemongrass (citral, flavonoids) may offer modest cardioprotective benefits, though clinical evidence in humans remains limited.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Lemongrass and pandan drink (香茅班兰茶) is a popular Southeast Asian herbal beverage widely consumed in Singapore, Malaysia, and Thailand. It is prepared by simmering fresh lemongrass stalks (Cymbopogon citratus) and pandan leaves (Pandanus amaryllifolius) in water, then straining and optionally sweetening with sugar or rock sugar. The resulting drink has a pale golden-green colour, a refreshing citrus-herbal aroma from the lemongrass, and a subtle sweet, vanilla-like fragrance from the pandan leaves. It may be served warm or chilled. In Singapore, it is commonly found in traditional coffee shops (kopitiams), hawker centres, and cafés such as The Light Cafe. Nutritionally, the drink is very low in calories (~10 kcal/100 ml), contains negligible protein and fat, and derives its small carbohydrate content (~2.5 g/100 ml) from added sugar in café preparations. The unsweetened base is essentially calorie-free. Lemongrass is a source of citral, a bioactive compound with reported antioxidant, antimicrobial, and mild anti-inflammatory properties. Pandan leaves contribute chlorophyll and 2-acetyl-1-pyrroline, the aromatic compound responsible for their characteristic fragrance. Clinically, this beverage is suitable for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, and cardiovascular disease when consumed without excessive added sugar. It is naturally vegan, gluten-free, dairy-free, and halal. The glycemic index is effectively zero for the unsweetened preparation.
common_user_queries: Is lemongrass drink good for diabetes?
What does pandan drink taste like?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for lemongrass-pandan drink was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for brewed herbal tea infusions and published compositional literature on lemongrass and pandan infusions. GI is estimated by analogy with herbal teas from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than low because the food is predominantly water with well-characterised trace nutrients, and the estimation uncertainty is low for a beverage of this type.

confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central herbal tea reference data. dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero as the plant material is strained out before serving. cholesterol_mg is naturally zero for a plant-based beverage. vitamin_b12_mcg is naturally zero as this is a plant-based beverage with no animal-derived ingredients. vitamin_d_mcg is naturally zero as this beverage contains no vitamin D sources. glycemic_index set to 1 (effectively zero) as this beverage contains no significant glycemic carbohydrates in its unsweetened form; gi_category low per convention for essentially zero-GI beverages. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, and trans_fat_g are 0.0 as confirmed zero for a fat-free herbal infusion. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reported in the reference source for this beverage type. vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the reference source. vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the reference source. vitamin_b3_niacin_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the reference source. vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the reference source. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the reference source. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the reference source. phosphorus_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the reference source. magnesium_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the reference source. zinc_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the reference source. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the reference source. Follow-up: HPB Singapore Foodo should be checked for any future addition of a lemongrass-pandan drink entry to upgrade confidence to high.
